Signals to the Executive The academics argue that these results ÔÇťpresent several implications for social governance in MacauÔÇŁ, given the existence of a ÔÇťparadox between residentsÔÇÖ subjective perceptions of crime and the decline reflected in official statisticsÔÇŁ. All of this ÔÇťunderlines the importance of reinforcing transparency in the dissemination of information and law enforcementÔÇŁ, with ÔÇťcrime-related information being disseminated through multiple channels and improving the visibility of prevention effortsÔÇŁ, which can ÔÇťcontribute to reducing fear and strengthening public trustÔÇŁ. The Government's actions in the area of ÔÇőÔÇősecurity seem to produce some results, the study describes, which is ÔÇťparticularly relevant in the context of rapid population agingÔÇŁ. ÔÇťIn addition to maintaining these investments, strengthening informal social control can also contribute to increasing the collective effectiveness of communities. Initiatives such as the expansion of volunteering programs and support for neighborhood associations (e.g. Federation of Residents' Associations of Macau; Association of Youth Volunteers of Macau) can provide additional social resources to mitigate risks associated with physical vulnerabilityÔÇŁ, it is argued.

item.title: UN | Human rights under large-scale attack
item.description: Human rights ÔÇťare under attack on a large scale throughout the worldÔÇŁ, carried out ÔÇťby those who hold the greatest powerÔÇŁ and, sometimes, even ÔÇťwith prideÔÇŁ, denounced the Secretary-General of the United Nations yesterday. "The rule of law is being replaced by the rule of force. And this attack does not come from the shadows nor is it a surprise. It is happening in plain sight and, often, led by those who hold the greatest power. All over the world, human rights are being deliberately and strategically repressed, and sometimes with pride", declared Guterres, speaking in Geneva at the opening session of the 61st session of the UN Human Rights Council. The secretary-general warned that the consequences of this attack ÔÇťare devastatingÔÇŁ, pointing out that people are suffering ÔÇťtwice: first with violence, oppression or exclusion, and then again with the worldÔÇÖs indifferenceÔÇŁ. "We live in a world where mass suffering is ignored, where human beings are used as bargaining chips, where international law is treated as a mere inconvenience. Conflicts multiply and impunity has become contagious. This is not due to a lack of knowledge, tools or institutions. It is the result of political choices", deplored the former Portuguese Prime Minister. According to Ant├│nio Guterres, ÔÇťthis crisis of respect for human rights is not an isolated caseÔÇŁ and ÔÇťreflects and magnifies all other global fracturesÔÇŁ, as ÔÇťhumanitarian needs are exploding, while financing is collapsingÔÇŁ, ÔÇťinequalities are increasing at an impressive speed, countries are drowning in debt and despair, and climate chaos is acceleratingÔÇŁ. The United Nations leader also highlighted that ÔÇťtechnology, especially artificial intelligence, is increasingly being used in ways that suppress rights, deepen inequality and expose marginalized people to new forms of discrimination, both online and offlineÔÇŁ. "On all fronts, those who are already vulnerable are being pushed further to the margins. And human rights defenders are among the first to be silenced when they try to warn us", he lamented, reinforcing that, "in this coordinated offensive, human rights are the first victims". Deplorable portrait Exposing a devastating portrait at a global level, the UN Secretary-General pointed out that today an ÔÇťincreasing tightening of civic spaceÔÇŁ is evident, with ÔÇťjournalists and activists imprisoned, NGOs [Non-Governmental Organizations] closed, women's rights in retreat, children's rights ignored, people with disabilities excluded and democracies in erosionÔÇŁ. "The right to peaceful assembly is crushed - and I once again condemn the recent violent crackdown on protests in Iran. Migrants are persecuted, detained and expelled, with complete disregard for their human rights and humanity. Refugees are made into scapegoats. LGBTIQ+ communities are vilified. Minorities and indigenous peoples are targeted. Religious communities are attacked," he continued. Alluding to his personal history, the last time he spoke as secretary-general at the opening of a session of the Human Rights Council, he pointed out that ÔÇťgrowing up under the Salazar dictatorshipÔÇŁ taught him that ÔÇťthe denial of human rights corrodes all aspects of society. ÔÇťWhen the Security Council is paralyzed, when vetoes serve as a political shield, when geopolitical rivalries prevail over the protection of civilians, the result is the same: impunity spreads, suffering multiplies and human rights are trampled on,ÔÇŁ he said. ÔÇťWe need a Security Council that reflects the world of today, not the world of 1945,ÔÇŁ he added.

item.title: Culture | There is a lack of public support to build a book market
item.description: Small market, little sales outside the territory and lack of support: book publishing in Macau faces problems in solidifying itself and competing with what is done abroad. Representative Lam Fat Iam recently defended policies to support the construction of a ÔÇťReading CityÔÇŁ. Pin-to Livros & M├║sica and the publisher Mandarina point out gaps that impede the dynamism of the sector It was in February, in an intervention before the agenda, that deputy Lam Fat Iam launched the challenge in the Legislative Assembly (AL): ÔÇťsupport the development of local physical bookstores and build a ÔÇśReading City'ÔÇŁ. In the text, read in the hemicycle, the deputy appointed by the Government argued that Macau, as the ÔÇťWorld Center for Tourism and LeisureÔÇŁ is a region where bookstores ÔÇťare not mere places to sell books, but also essential cultural spaces in communities, playing a crucial role in promoting the diversification of the tourism sector and sustainable development of the cityÔÇŁ. Lam Fat Iam knows what he's talking about. Born in July 1975, he has a PhD in History and presides over the Macau Cultural Education Publications Association, in addition to being director of the Faculty of Human and Social Sciences at the Macau Polytechnic University. In the same intervention, the person responsible suggested that ÔÇťspecific and precise support policiesÔÇŁ should be created, namely the creation of ÔÇťsupport programs for the exploration and cultural activities of bookstoresÔÇŁ, giving ÔÇťpriority to bookstores that promote Macau culture and regularly hold conferences and reading activitiesÔÇŁ. A system must also be created that encourages ÔÇťthe circulation and publication of local booksÔÇŁ, with ÔÇťpriority financing for the planning, editing and publication of books with Macau characteristicsÔÇŁ. HM questioned bookstores and publishers in order to understand the gaps in the Macau book market. Anson Ng, from Pin-to Livros & M├║sica, draws attention to the fact that ÔÇťthe market base, in itself, is small, with the book-reading population registering a decreaseÔÇŁ. Conversely, ÔÇťoperational costs remain high and there is competition from external online bookstore platformsÔÇŁ. Anson Ng further added that ÔÇťMacauÔÇÖs local publishers and local bookstores have not yet managed to form a self-sufficient, healthy and sustainable ecosystem, and that is precisely where supporting policies should focus their effortsÔÇŁ. Regarding the position of deputy Lam Fat Iam, the head of Pin-to Livros & M├║sica says that his ideas follow ÔÇťan acceptable directionÔÇŁ, however, ÔÇťan in-depth discussionÔÇŁ is necessary regarding ÔÇťdetails of implementationÔÇŁ of the ideas expressed in the interpellation. ÔÇťBefore establishing concrete support policies, we hope that relevant government departments can carefully study the experiences of other regionsÔÇŁ, in addition to maintaining ÔÇťextensive communication with the current industry in MacauÔÇŁ. For Anson Ng, we must also try to ÔÇťtruly understand the needs of the sectorÔÇŁ, as ÔÇťonly in this way will it be possible to develop transparent, fair and practical support policiesÔÇŁ. Contact difficulties Catarina Mesquita, founder of Mandarina, which publishes children's books, does not have a physical store, but sells her books in spaces such as Livraria Portuguesa. In response to HM, Catarina Mesquita suggests ÔÇťthat the Government should, together with schools, be able to place orders with bookstores and directly with publishersÔÇŁ, speaking of the specific case of ÔÇťNa RuaÔÇŁ, one of the Mandarina editions that she tried to place directly in schools. "The book 'Na Rua' talks about the history of Macau and I have tried, many times, for this book to be introduced in schools, not only because it has a history of the place where these children live, but also because it has many aspects of the history of Macau, and very specific characteristics [of the territory], which is talked about so much. And I have tried several times to contact the education services [Directorate of Education and Youth Development Services], I was unable to get that contact and it is very difficult. I have had people from the Government in presentation sessions about the Mandarin, but I didnÔÇÖt get any feedbackÔÇŁ, he laments. Catarina Mesquita therefore argues that the Government should ÔÇťtogether with schools, place orders with bookstores and directly with publishersÔÇŁ. "You end up falling into a hole that you don't really know what it is. This effort should be more valued, not in the sense of being praised, but in the sense of taking Macau's works and taking them into classrooms, which is the most efficient and effective way of getting them to children, in the case of children's literature. I think it is not something that is very difficult to do", he assumed. The subsidy issue It is true that the Executive has presented some measures to promote reading, and Lam Fat Iam noted this in his plenary intervention. ÔÇťIn recent years, the Macao SAR Government and society have already achieved results in promoting reading and building a ÔÇśReading City'ÔÇŁ, he described, particularly on the part of the Cultural Institute, with the creation of events such as the ÔÇťReading FestivalÔÇŁ, ÔÇťLibrary WeekÔÇŁ, ÔÇťMonth of Joint Reading throughout the City of MacauÔÇŁ and the ÔÇťReading Program for Babies and ChildrenÔÇŁ. There is, in his view, a ÔÇťsolid basis for a social environment favorable to readingÔÇŁ, but ÔÇťphysical bookstores, as essential spaces for reading, face survival difficultiesÔÇŁ, highlighted the deputy. Lam Fat Iam also highlighted ÔÇťthe limited size of the market, high rental costs and a fragile chain of production and sales of local publications created an environment with ÔÇśdifficulty in publishing, buying and selling books'ÔÇŁ. Something that, in the deputy's opinion, ÔÇťmakes it difficult for many valuable Macau books to reach the hands of residents and visitorsÔÇŁ. Catarina Mesquita also draws attention to the fact that Macau has many published books and is ÔÇťa very rich territoryÔÇŁ in terms of its history and culture, but that more needs to be done beyond the simple subsidy to publish a book. In the case of Mandarina, the first five books were made ÔÇťwithout any subsidies or supportÔÇŁ. "Perhaps due to a personality trait, I didn't want Mandarina, in the beginning, to be the result of support or subsidies. I wanted what I achieved to be done by me, and that made me work much harder", he assures. Catarina Mesquita therefore feels that the financial support, or this initial subsidy, ÔÇťis enough to produce a book, and thatÔÇÖs itÔÇŁ, when, in fact, ÔÇťthis support is more important later in terms of distribution, ordering, people being able to have the book in their handsÔÇŁ. And he argues that more should be done to support the distribution of works. ÔÇťThere is no support, or bridgeÔÇŁ in this sense, he considers. "There is so much talk about the platform with Portuguese-speaking countries, but there is no support for the distribution and transport of books to regions other than Macau, and this is fundamental. I have come across this problem, because at the moment Mandarina is starting its first steps in Portugal and I have no way of getting the books to the country without having any type of support." In the responsible's view, the connection between Portugal and the territory could be explored more effectively. "In terms of laws and taxes we pay on imports, a partnership that would make us benefit from it made sense. Therefore, I think there is a hole here that needs to be resolved quickly." The four measures Lam Fat Iam also left, in his intervention, suggestions to improve the distribution system for editorial works, defending the construction of a ÔÇťvirtuous system for the circulation and publication of local booksÔÇŁ, with ÔÇťpriority financing for the planning, editing and publication of books with Macau characteristicsÔÇŁ. Regarding ÔÇťpublic services, public libraries, community libraries and primary and secondary schools financed by the Government, must place orders for books in local bookstoresÔÇŁ. Furthermore, in connection with urban renewal projects, the deputy believes that the ÔÇťBookstore +ÔÇŁ model can be developed, defining ÔÇťguidelinesÔÇŁ and reserving ÔÇťcultural spaces to encourage the integration of bookstores with cafes, art galleries, cultural and creative stores and even revitalization projects for historic buildingsÔÇŁ. For the deputy, bookstores must be ÔÇťmultifunctional cultural landmarks that integrate reading, leisure, exhibitions and cultural exchangeÔÇŁ, and there must also be support for participation in local and regional book fairs. HM contacted other bookstores in Macau, but at the time of writing this edition had received no further responses.

item.title: Rota das Letras | Film by Filipa Queiroz tells the story of homeless people from Alta de Coimbra
item.description: ÔÇťSalatinas ÔÇô Stories from the Old Alta of CoimbraÔÇŁ brings journalist Filipa Queiroz back to Macau, together with Rafael Vieira and Tiago Cerveira, co-authors of the documentary that is part of the Rota das Letras poster. The film tells the story of how, during the Estado Novo, around three thousand residents were expelled from Alta de Coimbra to allow the construction of the University City The University City in Alta de Coimbra is one of the city's tourist attractions, offering a stunning landscape. However, despite the beauty, the history of the construction of Cidade Universit├íria is full of suffering. In the documentary ÔÇťSalatinas ÔÇô Hist├│rias da Velha Alta de CoimbraÔÇŁ, Filipa Queiroz, Rafael Vieira and Tiago Cerveira tell the story of the people who lived there in the 1940s and who found themselves suddenly homeless and without a plan B. The film is part of the poster for the Rota das Letras Literary Festival, which starts on March 5th. ÔÇťWe hope that, in Macau, the story can have an impact on everyone and in particular on the many people from Coimbra who we know reside in the territoryÔÇŁ, Filipa Queiroz, who lived in Macau for several years, told HM and TDM ÔÇô Macau Television. He is happy to return and to reveal this story unknown to many. And he gives the greatest praise to a festival that saw its birth 15 years ago. "I find the programming of this edition of Rota das Letras particularly courageous and relevant taking into account the contexts of Portugal, Macau and the world at the moment. We try to present the film where it makes sense and this is precisely the case. It is a film about people, but also about power, resistance, identity and the price of progress ÔÇô themes that Macau knows so well", he highlighted. Filipa Queiroz came into contact with the story of these people through a report. ÔÇťRafael Vieira collaborated with me as a journalist, as he ran a publication in Coimbra [Coimbra Coolectiva] and told me that there was an [interesting] story there, and that he wanted to tell stories outside the box, more invisible, so to speak.ÔÇŁ ÔÇťSalatinasÔÇŁ was born, in reference to the name given to the people who lived in Alta de Coimbra, where there were already faculties of Literature, Law and the library. However, the Estado Novo evicted these people to build the currently existing buildings. Three thousand people left their homes, making this ÔÇťone of the biggest urban transformations in Portugal, at a time when forced evictions, lack of access to housing and social segregation still make headlinesÔÇŁ, describes the documentaryÔÇÖs synopsis. ÔÇťSalatinasÔÇŁ is, therefore, ÔÇťa trip down memory lane of the historic neighborhood wiped off the map under the regimeÔÇÖs impetus for renewal and the hubbub of students, tricanas, grocers and tailors that hides beneath the collegesÔÇŁ, adds the same synopsis. Leave quickly ÔÇťWhat happened was the will of the Government at that timeÔÇŁ, says Filipa Queiroz, to explain how a political decision changed the lives of so many people. "The University of Coimbra moved between Lisbon and Coimbra for many years, but then it was located in Coimbra and was the only one in the country, where the country's brains, the elite, were. There was then a decision that the university needed to grow and it had to be there. Salazar himself had studied there [Faculty of Law], in addition to there being a hospital. It wasn't about requalification, but they really destroyed what there was to build on top." What followed was a profound transformation in families in the area. Many never fully recovered. "When the destruction began, basically the Government warned the people who lived there that they all had to get out of there. They gave values ÔÇőÔÇőto the houses [for compensation], completely disproportionate and much lower than the value of the houses." "There is a person we mention in the film who was part of Salazar's circle, she was a doctor and an illustrious person, and she managed to have her house be one of the last to be removed, but she was still [homeless]. The poorer and more humble the people were, the quicker they had to leave, with one hand in front, the other behind. These people were thrown into neighborhoods of the city, which we can't even call neighborhoods, because it was something improvised at the time", says the co-author of the documentary. We are talking about areas with ÔÇťfragileÔÇŁ houses. However, the precariousness of housing already came from behind. "Little houses were built that weren't of incredible quality, but the houses in Alta de Coimbra weren't there either, many of them. There was no basic sanitation, that needed to be requalified." A ÔÇťself-identityÔÇŁ Filipa Queiroz highlights how ÔÇťthe solution was to end everything, not valuing the fact that it is a neighborhood, and with its own identity, and this is the main issueÔÇŁ. The neighborhood was Salatina. "Salatinas are people who worked or lived in the upper part of the city, or who were born there, and who crossed paths daily with the student elite. This diversity was very rich, which ended up having effects on the cultural aspect, on music, for example." Coimbra is a land associated with names in Portuguese music, especially interventionists, such as Zeca Afonso and Carlos Paredes, and among the ÔÇťdoctorsÔÇŁ there were also ÔÇťthe futricasÔÇŁ, that is, ÔÇťthe people of Coimbra, but no one ever remembered those peopleÔÇŁ. Filipa Queiroz highlights that ÔÇťmany people, including those from Coimbra, donÔÇÖt know what happened thereÔÇŁ. ÔÇťThere was a city there, a very concentrated urban network full of people and life, with S├úo Jo├úo bonfires, cultural events, all very identity-related things.ÔÇŁ All things destroyed with the requalification and expansion of the University City. Filipa Queiroz highlights that nowadays, and despite university experiences, the city's Alta no longer has the dynamics it once had. ÔÇťIt has that austere architecture and at night, nothing happens.ÔÇŁ ÔÇťSalatinasÔÇŁ gave voice to a topic that is rarely talked about. "People are very moved and feel very valued. We had people who went to the premiere [in Coimbra, in the Alta area] and who told us: 'It seems that there has been a repair here'. This even led to the death of some people, because they committed suicide out of grief, because they had to leave the place where they built their business and their lives". Filipa Queiroz also thanks Ricardo Pinto, director of Rota das Letras, for the invitation made. "He invited us knowing only the idea of ÔÇőÔÇőthe film. This will allow my colleagues to discover, for the first time, this place, Macau, which is so special to me." Filipa Queiroz wants to continue telling stories through images. Having made documentaries linked to Macau, such as ÔÇťBoat PeopleÔÇŁ and ÔÇťOnce Upon a Time in Ka-H├│ÔÇŁ, the idea is to continue in an audiovisual genre so close to journalism. "Documentaryism adds an artistic layer and time that journalism does not allow. Yes, I would like to make [more documentaries], that is always on my horizon. There are always challenges, with financing the work and the time that can be dedicated to it, but without a doubt, yes, I would like to continue."

item.title: Heritage | Stories of the stores on Rua Cinco de Outubro told in a book
item.description: Natalie Fu has just released a book about traditional commerce on Rua Cinco de Outubro, at the invitation of the Macau Cultural Heritage Reinvention Studies Association. ÔÇťMacaoÔÇÖs Historical House Taxonomy ÔÇô The Shophouses at the Rua Cinco de OutubroÔÇŁ tells the centuries-old story of shophouses that were the ÔÇťheart of MacauÔÇÖs Chinese communityÔÇŁ A book has just been published that reveals a little about the history of commerce carried out by the Chinese community on Rua Cinco de Outubro, located in Porto Interior, the scene of many commercial movements over the centuries. The work also explains how these stores, which are at the same time houses, reveal details about the history of Macau. ÔÇťMacaoÔÇÖs Historical House Taxonomy ÔÇô The Shophouses at the Rua Cinco de OutubroÔÇŁ, written by Natalie Fu, a doctoral student at the University of S├úo Jos├ę, was invited by the Association for Studies on the Reinvention of Cultural Heritage of Macau, whose edition also depends on ÔÇťthe support and assistance of many other peopleÔÇŁ, the author told HM. One of the book's sales outlets, which does not have a Portuguese version, is Pin-to Livros. ÔÇťThe investigation began with the love for Macau and its old buildingsÔÇŁ, reveals the author, who began the research work around these buildings in 2022, in the middle of the pandemic. "Rua de Cinco de Outubro has always been an important commercial area in Macau. Once, it was the heart of Macau's Chinese community. Even today, many old stores remain close to the local neighborhood. The stores are mixed-use buildings", explains the author. This is the stage for commercial diversity, as Rua Cinco de Outubro ÔÇťis home to several businesses, such as traditional Chinese bakeries, Chinese medicine stores, tea shops and fruit shopsÔÇŁ. According to the presentation of the work, made on social media by the association itself, this type of property constitutes ÔÇťa common type of multifunctional residential buildingÔÇŁ, where the ÔÇťplan of the buildings is generally rectangular, with two to three floorsÔÇŁ. Typically, the ground floor is used for the store or as a warehouse, while the upper floors are reserved for housing. "This type of construction can adapt to the local climate, presenting great flexibility in the construction methods and materials used. Due to the diversity of daily uses, shop houses were once the main type of building on the streets of Macau, having important historical, social, cultural, architectural and aesthetic value", it further reads. Preservation Challenges Still according to the same note, the book draws attention to the importance of preserving this type of construction and, consequently, a symbol of local heritage. ÔÇťUnfortunately, because they are small-scale and scattered buildings, and because they are not fully protected by the Macau Heritage Protection Law, they have been gradually disappearing with the rapid development of societyÔÇŁ, and, currently, ÔÇťonly a small number of these buildings have the classification of ÔÇśmonumentÔÇÖ and are protectedÔÇŁ. For Natalie Fu, ÔÇťthe preservation of Macau's stores faces many challenges, one of which is the fact that they are not included in the existing list of protected old buildings in MacauÔÇŁ, in addition to that, in her view, ÔÇťchanges in construction laws have also made it difficult to maintain these old buildingsÔÇŁ. Asked about the future of this iconic street in Porto Interior, the author recognizes that ÔÇťthe growth of a city inevitably involves the renovation and disappearance of buildingsÔÇŁ, so ÔÇťdetermining which buildings should be preserved and how to preserve them is a long-term challengeÔÇŁ. Natalie Fu even talks about the need to extend the investigation to more streets in Macau that are also traditional shopping areas. "Research and documentation of the stores on Rua Cinco de Outubro and in Macau are urgent as their number is dwindling. Additionally, additional academic investigations and discussions with the local community are needed to inform the future direction of the Inner Harbor area." In relation to the panorama on Rua Cinco de Outubro, the author describes that maintenance should be focused on, keeping commercial spaces open. "Stores that are kept in use better highlight their social value. Taking some stores on Rua Cinco de Outubro as an example, the existence of century-old stores further emphasizes the cultural and historical value of the area and the stores. Furthermore, 'keeping them in use' allows for more immediate monitoring and maintenance, which is the most direct way of protecting ancient architecture." A certain influence In a territory characterized by the coexistence of Portuguese and Chinese cultures, Natalie Fu's book shows how these houses were influenced by other cultures. ÔÇťChinese and Western architecture in Macau have always influenced each other throughout historical development, and this feature is particularly visible in the appearance of the shop houses.ÔÇŁ In this way, this book helps to cement this characteristic, ÔÇťestablishing a classification system for MacauÔÇÖs shop houses, consolidating their architectural definitionÔÇŁ. This book is, therefore, a ÔÇťclassifying study of the old shop houses in MacauÔÇŁ, constituting ÔÇťthe first step towards their safeguardingÔÇŁ, describes, in the same note, the association that supported the editorial and research project. "The author carried out a detailed record and survey [of the stores], starting with visits and cartography surveys in the Porto Interior area. Maps were drawn up of the location of the shop houses on that street, analysis sheets based on fieldwork, photographic records and interviews with current traders and residents, in order to understand the history of the use of each building." Furthermore, the work documents seven cases in particular, ÔÇťproviding information on their history, evolution and current use, as well as a preliminary discussion on possible preservation directionsÔÇŁ. The book also records two examples of shop houses that have now disappeared. ÔÇťIt is hoped that this work will encourage more people to discover the ÔÇśbeauty of Macau.ÔÇÖ With the shop houses as a central theme, it is expected to promote exchange and discussion between academics from different areasÔÇŁ, the association also points out. Natalie Fu invited ÔÇťseveral experts and academics to share their ideas, experiences and suggestions on the protection and future of this type of buildingsÔÇŁ, it is described. Born and raised in Macau, Natalie Fu had the opportunity to visit all of Southeast Asia from a young age, trips that led her to ÔÇťfoster a deep appreciation and passion for architectureÔÇŁ. The author has a degree in Architecture, also describing herself as a person interested in the area of ÔÇőÔÇőheritage conservation and a ÔÇťhistorical architecture scholar trained in MacauÔÇŁ.

item.title: Diaspora | Greater connection between Casa de Macau and Casa de Goa defended
item.description: The conference ÔÇťCasa de Goa and Casa de Macau: fundamental agents of an indispensable dialogueÔÇŁ took place in Lisbon yesterday, which analyzed the connecting role that the entities can have in the Goan and Macau diaspora. Carlos Piteira, president of Casa de Macau, highlights the importance of creating bridges with Casa de Goa through the ÔÇťparallel representation of Portuguese descendantsÔÇŁ What role can Casa de Macau and Casa de Goa, in Lisbon, have, taking into account the passage of time and the developments brought by the new generations of the Macanese and Goan communities? It is true that Macau and Goa have long ceased to be part of the former Portuguese empire, but sociocultural ties remain. With this in mind, the lecture ÔÇťCasa de Goa and Casa de Macau: fundamental agents of an indispensable dialogueÔÇŁ took place yesterday at the Lisbon Geography Society, which sought to outline answers to the challenges of the future. To HM, Carlos Piteira, president of the Casa de Macau in Lisbon, declared that ÔÇťit would be interesting if the bridges between the Casa de Macau and the Casa de Goa could also be considered in parallel with their representation of Portuguese descendants and mixed culturesÔÇŁ, taking into account that the two entities ÔÇťare fundamental pillars for the representation of these communitiesÔÇŁ. The person responsible, himself Macanese, highlights ÔÇťthe similarities of their gastronomy, Creole and festivitiesÔÇŁ between the two communities. It would therefore be ÔÇťinteresting to combine the small representations of the giants of the Asian world, represented by the symbolism of the Dragon and the Elephant, as structuring elements for a dialogue between Portugal and Asia, political and institutional wills permitÔÇŁ. In Carlos Piteira's view, the two houses ÔÇťreinforce the legitimized heritage of the dialogue between the West and the East, and Portugal's centuries-old relations with these countries, often ignored and forgotten by official and institutional powersÔÇŁ. Pedro Cola├žo, from the management of Casa de Goa, highlights the importance of preserving the memory of the Portuguese presence both in Goa and in the remaining territories of Daman and Diu. "There have been 450 years of history, in the case of Macau it was a few more decades, and we try to keep up with current Goan culture. One of the most important parts of our work is to build a bridge between Goa and Lisbon, and on a daily basis we contact artists, musicians and members of the community, that is our role", he described to HM. Pedro Cola├žo gave the example of the concert that will take place on March 8th at Funda├ž├úo Oriente, in Lisbon, entitled ÔÇťOscar Castellino ÔÇô Voz de ├ôpera de Goa and Piano with Rodrigo AyalaÔÇŁ. Furthermore, Casa de Goa, currently without its own headquarters, seeks, like Casa de Macau, to attract new members, especially from the new generations, in order to continue the project, so that the Casa remains ÔÇťas a hub of unity for Goans and, increasingly, for friends of GoaÔÇŁ. Follow the story Casa de Macau was founded in Lisbon in 1966 and has followed different phases not only in the history of Portugal but also in the country's relationship with Macau and China. "The existence of the House of Macau undoubtedly marks a parallel with the history of Portugal itself for more than 70 years. Macau was the 'pearl of the East' for the Estado Novo and lived through the turmoil of the revolutionary period of April 1974, adapting and establishing itself as the 'bridge between the West and East'". Finally, after the transition to the People's Republic of China, we have seen the reorganization [of Macau] as a central territory in the connection, from China, with Portuguese-speaking countries.ÔÇŁ Carlos Piteira also described that the House of Macau has followed ÔÇťthe history of the times, expanding the vision of the Portuguese presence in this territoryÔÇŁ. Furthermore, he had a ÔÇťno less importantÔÇŁ task, having ÔÇťcrystallized, protected and perpetuated the Macanese community in Portugal and the worldÔÇŁ. Currently, Casa de Macau has developed several activities that bring more members to Avenida Gago Coutinho, where it is located, and whose building has recently undergone restoration work. Every Wednesday there are Macanese food lunches with Chef Tina, originally from Macau, as well as film sessions and lectures. The Casa's mission is currently ÔÇťto carry out activities to promote Macau and Macanese cultureÔÇŁ, providing ÔÇťsupport and promotion of studies and works of scientific content about Macau, Macau peopleÔÇŁ and also providing ÔÇťinstitutional interconnection with other entities linked to Macau, Portugal and Casas de Macau in the diasporaÔÇŁ. Who is from Goa and who is not Pedro Cola├žo explained that the Goan community in Lisbon ÔÇťhas an easier time talking to the remaining Indian diasporaÔÇŁ in Portugal, taking into account that, for example, ÔÇťthe connection is very oldÔÇŁ with the Hindu community. ÔÇťWe are sorry that sometimes [the community] is not talked about more in the media, because it is an important part of Portuguese historyÔÇŁ, laments the person in charge, remembering prominent figures in the country, such as Ant├│nio Costa, who was Portuguese prime minister, or Narana Coissor├│, former president of Casa de Goa and former deputy of the CDS-PP. ÔÇťThere are more notorious figures, but in general the Goan community has integrated itself very well and is known for being a very educated communityÔÇŁ, he also recalled, regretting that in Portugal it is not possible to make a distinction between communities of Indian origin. "One thing that is worrying is the lack of knowledge that exists in relation to the communities. Nowadays there is a lot of pressure due to immigration and then everything gets mixed up", highlights Pedro Cola├žo, remembering that there is a strong feeling in being Goan, particularly in connection with Catholicism and a very unique culture as opposed to Hindustani communities that are currently very present in Portugal. "The Goan community is so mixed that half of my family, or more, do not appear to be Goan, but I, by chance, do. There is a feeling of being Goan, something that unites us. The other communities of the Indian diaspora are often confessional, they have an associated religion, while the Goan community is, for the most part, Catholic, but everyone goes to their own church, it is very spread out", he pointed out. The future is a challenge Pedro Cola├žo speaks of a ÔÇťchallenging futureÔÇŁ for Casa de Goa. "I'm now in my second term, the leadership has changed president, and one of the things we've been trying to do is to bring young people into the governing bodies. We now have three or four younger members, under the age of 40. We've also been trying to expand the House to non-Goans." In this point, there are similarities with the Casa de Macau, which has also sought to expand the range of members to Chinese or Portuguese people who do not belong to the community. Pedro Cola├žo adds that, over the years, things have changed. "30 years ago the community was very large, but at this moment, and like all institutions, we have to expand. There are many people who really like Goa and who know a lot about the territory, perhaps even more than me, and they are the ones who have to be pulled", he assumed. Currently, Casa de Goa has 500 members and, even without a headquarters, it seeks to carry out activities, particularly in partnership with Funda├ž├úo Oriente. A monthly bulletin is published. They no longer have headquarters due to the metro works in Lisbon, which has made the House's agenda difficult. ÔÇťWe are in the process of finding a new headquarters, and it is something that has been a constraint, but we try to carry out as many activities as possible and, every month, we always have activitiesÔÇŁ, concluded Pedro Cola├žo.

item.title: IPIM | Trade fair for Portuguese-speaking countries in October
item.description: The Government announced yesterday that it will organize a trade fair in October dedicated only to Portuguese-speaking countries, instead of the usual event that brings together companies from China and Portuguese-speaking markets. The Macau Trade and Investment Promotion Institute (IPIM) launched a competition to coordinate the Exhibition of Products and Services of Portuguese Speaking Countries (Macau) 2026 (PLPEX), scheduled for between 21 and 24 October. The specifications foresee that PLPEX will occupy an area of ÔÇőÔÇősix thousand square meters, with around 200 stands for exhibitors ÔÇťin line with Macau's positioning as a Sino-Lusophone platformÔÇŁ. The fair aims to attract at least 200 exhibitors, of which 10 percent must be local companies, 10 percent from Spain and 80 percent from Portuguese-speaking markets, ÔÇťit being mandatory to attract the participation of companies from all nine Portuguese-speaking countriesÔÇŁ. The agricultural sector must represent at least 30 percent of all exhibitors, cross-border electronic commerce 20 percent and the blue economy, linked to the sea, at least 10 percent, states in the specifications. The PLPEX organizer must also create a campaign to promote the fair in Portugal and Spain. Lusa questioned IPIM about whether the new fair will replace the China-Portuguese Speaking Countries Economic and Commercial Exhibition (C-PLPEX), which was organized in October 2025, in parallel with the Macau International Fair (MIF, its acronym in English), but did not receive any response.

item.title: Non-Residents | Ip Sio Kai wants the Government to facilitate new hiring
item.description: The deputy argues that the economy is expanding and that companies are facing constraints. Therefore, Ip defends the ease of hiring non-residents, thinking about companies that win public tenders ┬á Deputy Ip Sio Kai defends the facilitation of mechanisms for hiring non-resident workers, thinking about the awarding of public contracts. The position of the deputy general director of the Macau Branch of the Bank of China and president of the Macau Banks Association was taken through a written interpellation. Despite justifying the question with the constraints on the expansion of local companies, Ip Sio Kai mainly relates the problem of lack of labor with public contracts and authorizations to hire non-resident workers. In these cases, the legislator points out that work permits can only be requested after the presentation of public procurement contracts. However, public competitions are considered ÔÇťslowÔÇŁ processes and hiring non-residents ÔÇťtakes around three monthsÔÇŁ. Therefore, Ip Sio Kai explains that there is always a risk that contracts for works require an immediate start, without there being available labor, which puts ÔÇťpressure on the fulfillment of these contractsÔÇŁ. In view of the problem, Ip wants to know whether the Government will create a conditional approval mechanism for the hiring of non-residents, in which authorizations are approved in advance, but only confirmed after the award of works or other work. "This would allow companies to submit applications from non-resident workers before formally securing the award. Based on reasonable commercial projections, authorities would first inform companies about the number of non-resident workers that could be approved if they win the award", he argued. ┬á Strategic areas Ip Sio Kai also asks that the policy for authorizing the hiring of non-residents becomes clearer and areas are identified in which procedures are more simplified. The deputy therefore wants to know whether ÔÇťcategories of professions most likelyÔÇŁ to see requests approved quickly will be published. The member of the Legislative Assembly argues that a publication of this kind would ÔÇťincrease industry confidence in policies relating to non-resident workers, given the persistent shortage of local labor or the low availability to work in certain professionsÔÇŁ. At the same time, the deputy asks for greater flexibility in relation to quotas for non-resident workers that are not used for a certain period of time. Currently, if companies obtain a quota but do not use it within the six-month period, that is, they do not hire the non-resident worker, the quota is cancelled. Ip Sio Kai argues that this deadline should be increased, due to the ÔÇťintensification of regional competitionÔÇŁ.

item.title: Justice | Lawyers for all defendants in the Lee Sio Kuan case deny crimes
item.description: Lee Sio Kuan and Wu U Choi are accused by the Public Prosecutor's Office of 48 crimes of document forgery and 235 crimes of electoral corruption The 18 defendants in the electoral corruption case involving the Ou Mun Kong I list, candidate for the 2021 legislative elections, refused to have committed any crime. The position was taken at the Base Judicial Court (TJB), on Monday, by defense lawyers, during the final arguments of the trial. In turn, all the defendants chose to remain silent. Lee Sio Kuan, leader and leader of Ou Mun Kong I, and Wu U Choi are accused of 48 crimes of document forgery and 235 crimes of electoral corruption. Che Mio Peng and the remaining 15 defendants were charged with the crime of electoral corruption. According to the Public Prosecutor's Office (MP), Lee Sio Kuan financed, organized and offered a half-day tour to more than 200 residents, which included a tour guide, meals and gifts with slogans supporting the list. The prosecution considers that the purpose of offering the trip was to get voters to sign the list form, as part of the collection of 300 signatures to constitute the candidacy committee, delivered to the Electoral Affairs Committee of the Legislative Assembly (CAEAL). Lee Sio Kuan's defense lawyer considered that his client is not guilty of the crime of document forgery, because he acted negligently, without intent. Given the high number of forms supporting the candidacy committee, the lawyer acknowledged that Lee acted carelessly, and that if he had been aware of the existence of forged signatures he would have prevented them from being submitted. Regarding the accusations of electoral corruption, the lawyer argued that the excursion was not premeditated, and that Lee sio Kuan did not directly ask or order other people to organize the excursion. The roles of Wu and Che From the MP's perspective, Wu U Choi and Che Mio Peng were also fundamental to the trip, as they helped with the organization and invited residents to participate in the event. However, Wu U Choi's lawyer rejected this version. According to the agent, his client carried out the excursion based on a strictly commercial logic, unaware of the reasons and objectives of the organization. The lawyer stated that the evidence presented throughout the trial by the MP regarding signature forgery is insufficient to prove the commission of the crimes. In turn, Che Mio Peng's lawyer revealed that the defendant did not know Lee Sio Kuan well, and that her involvement in the excursions was solely due to the fact that she had a network of knowledge linked to tourism in the Interior. Che's defender also stated that his client only acted upon a request from Wu U Choi, and that she never intended to influence residents to sign the forms establishing the candidacy committee. Che Mio Peng's lawyer also stated that the client did not sign the form nor did she receive direct benefits. In relation to the defense of the remaining defendants, the lawyers justified their participation in the trips and the signing of the forms with little education from their clients, many of whom were elderly, with a low level of education, and some were even illiterate, who were unable to understand the consequences of their actions. The sentence is scheduled to be read on April 10th.

item.title: Leong Sun Iok wants sporting events that connect Macau to the interior
item.description: Deputy Leong Sun Iok defends the organization of more international sporting events that connect Macau, Hong Kong and Mainland China. The position was taken through a written interpellation, and is based on the holding of the National Games, in the second half of last year. According to the legislator of the Federation of Workers' Associations of Macau (FAOM), in an effort to promote Macau as an international city, the territory should take advantage of the experience of the National Games, when a cycling race was organized that crossed Macau, Hong Kong and the province of Guangdong, covering a length of 231.8 kilometers. Leong Sun Iok also mentions the example of the marathon that took place between Hong Kong and Shenzhen, among examples of events with international potential. ÔÇťSociety in general is eager to see whether these valuable pioneering experiences can be transformed, after the event, into institutionalized advantages, genuinely promoting the development of Macau as a Sports City, while deepening regional cooperation between the three regionsÔÇŁ, he considered. ÔÇťWhen introducing or hosting future international sporting events, will there be conditions to take advantage of the experience gained at the National Games?ÔÇŁ, he asks. ÔÇťMore specifically, will a mechanism be established to facilitate the authorization of sporting events in cooperation with Guangdong and Hong Kong?ÔÇŁ, he asks. Greater facilities Leong also points out that holding sporting events between different regions ÔÇťwould provide greater scope for planning the scale and routes of sporting events in MacauÔÇŁ. At the same time, it indicates that in recent years conditions have been created for greater circulation in the Greater Bay Area, which can benefit the Macao SAR in becoming an international city. Holding events in Macau in cooperation with Hong Kong and Guangdong is not new. However, the National Games allowed crossing borders during parts of the competition route. For years, the South China Sea Tour cycling race took place, with stages in different cities in what is currently the Greater Bay Area. For example, in 2008, the race started in Hong Kong, where the first stage took place entirely, but in the following days it crossed the border, with stages held in the cities of Shenzhen, Dongguan, Guangzhou, Zhonghsan and Zhuhai. That year, the race ended in Macau with Portuguese Micael Isidoro winning the stage that was entirely contested in Macau. Chinese Xu Gang was the overall winner.

item.title: Maternity | Associations warn of license costs
item.description: The third session of the public consultation on changes to the labor relations law took place this Tuesday, with associations warning of the increase in costs associated with maternity leave. According to the Ou Mun newspaper, the vice-president of the General Women's Association of Macau, Ao Ieong Ut Seng, said that the proposal to increase the leave to 90 days is appropriate, but situations that could worsen women's working conditions and scenarios of employment discrimination should be avoided, taking into account that many small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) face economic difficulties. Ao Ieong Ut Seng suggested that there is more support for women who have twin children, in which case they can enjoy more days of maternity leave. For her part, the vice-president of the General Union of Residents' Associations of Macau, Cheong Sok Leng, pointed out that SMEs have limited resources to support the costs of maternity leaves and that the Government should continue to grant subsidies to alleviate part of the companies' costs, a subsidy that covers 20 days of leave.

item.title: Two Sessions | Sam Hou Fai and Macao SAR representatives in Beijing
item.description: A delegation of Macau government officials and representatives from the Two Sessions left for Beijing yesterday. Among its priorities are the alignment of the national 15th Five-Year Plan with the Macao SAR's 3rd Five-Year Plan, and the development of the Deep Cooperation Zone in Hengqin The Chief Executive left yesterday for Beijing, where he will stay until Thursday, to attend the opening of the Fourth Session of the 14th National People's Congress (NPC), the Social Communication Office reported yesterday. In the absence of Sam Hou Fai, the Secretary for Administration and Justice, Wong Sio Chak, will act as Chief Executive on an interim basis. The Macau delegation also included representatives of the Macao SAR in the National People's Congress (NPC) and the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC). Before leaving, some officials highlighted as priorities the alignment of the national 15th Five-Year Plan with the Macao SAR's 3rd Five-Year Plan, as well as the integration and development of the Deep Cooperation Zone in Hengqin. Deputy Kevin Ho, representative at the National People's Assembly, highlighted the national 15th Five-Year Plan as the main focus of the Two Sessions and the need for articulation with the Macao SAR's long-term political direction. Kevin Ho revealed that this year the Macau delegation will submit at least six individual and two collective proposals to the APN, focusing on the integration of services between Macau, the Greater Bay Area and Hengqin. Speaking to the newspaper Ou Mun, the deputy and nephew of the first Chief Executive of the Macao SAR also stated that the 15th National Games went beyond the sporting sphere, demonstrating the ability to overcome barriers that were previously erected due to cross-border difficulties. As such, he stated that the sporting event should serve as a motto for greater regional integration in terms of sharing data and information. One voice Delegate and former congresswoman Chan Hong highlighted the same priorities. After focusing on infrastructure and connection mechanisms between the Macao SAR and Hengqin, Chan Hong indicated that integration in the Greater Bay Area has entered a new phase that will involve promoting ÔÇťheart-to-heart connectivityÔÇŁ to facilitate cross-border life for residents. On the educational front, the representative once again highlighted the importance of investing in nationalist education. Regarding Macau's representatives at the CPPCC, Frederico Ma highlighted the training and recruitment of professional staff as a priority and the role of the International University City of Hengqin in achieving this objective. Deputy Iau Teng Pio also highlighted the role that the university campus on Ilha da Montanha will have in the future of the Macao SAR.

item.title: National Security | Fired public servants can appeal
item.description: A public servant who is dismissed for posing a risk to national security may appeal to the courts. The State Security Defense Commission will have an internal mechanism for controlling and managing expenses, which will be reported to the Legislative Assembly A public servant who is fired after being considered by the State Security Defense Commission (CDSE) to be a risk to China's national security will be able to appeal, a legal advisor to the Legislative Assembly (AL) said yesterday. Leong Sun Iok, the deputy who chairs the AL committee that is analyzing the proposed law on the CDSE regime, recalled that there is no possible appeal for the decisions and opinions of this body. However, the deputy added that, in a meeting held yesterday, the Secretary for Security, Chan Tsz King, agreed to change the proposal, which was generally approved unanimously on February 10. ÔÇťWhether or not decisions taken by other entities based on the [CDSE] opinion are challengeable or appealable depends on the specific provisions of each law in question,ÔÇŁ explained Leong, including the Public Administration Workers Statute. When asked by Lusa whether an employee who had been disciplined or dismissed by the public service to which he belongs, after receiving a negative opinion from the CDSE, could appeal or not, the deputy did not respond. But Vu Ka Vai, an AL legal advisor, confirmed to Lusa that the statute allows public service workers to appeal disciplinary decisions, including to the courts. The advisor added that, in practice, the CDSE will have the final say on whether or not trials linked to national security will be held behind closed doors. Question of self-control It was also revealed that an internal mechanism for controlling and managing CDSE expenses will be created, accounts that will appear in an annual report submitted to the AL. In compliance with the stipulations of the diploma that was unanimously approved, on February 12th, Leong Sun Iok indicated that the funds that will support the expenses of the State Security Defense Commission will be taken from the Macao SAR's ordinary revenue. Regarding the expense report that will be delivered to deputies, it will be up to the legislative body to decide the extent of the information that will be made available to the public. Leong Sun Iok added that Government representatives did not provide an estimate for the operating expenses of the CDSE and that the deputies' main concerns were related to the degree of transparency and the information that will be public. Jo├úo Luz / Lusa

item.title: ÔÇťThree regionsÔÇŁ events: from pioneering to institutionalization
item.description:
By Manuel Silv├ęrio ÔÇô former president of the Sports Institute and co-founder of the Macau Olympic Committee ┬á The written question from Deputy Leong Sun Iok raises a timely and, above all, concrete point: how to transform recent experiences ÔÇö in particular the cycling event that linked Macau, Hong Kong and Guangdong ÔÇö into institutionalized advantages, capable of reinforcing regional cooperation and consolidating Macau as a Sports City. The merit of this position also lies in the profile of those who place it. Leong Sun Iok is a young deputy, close to the field, who regularly accompanies athletes and practitioners. And it is not insignificant that it is linked to the Federation of Workers' Associations of Macau (FAOM), an institution with strong social and sporting traditions, where many young people ÔÇö and also ÔÇťanonymousÔÇŁ practitioners ÔÇö have found, over decades, space to train, compete and create active lifestyle habits. Just remember the emblematic Campo dos Oper├írios, in the heart of the city, next to the Portuguese School, where the Grand Lisboa stands today ÔÇö a symbol of how Macau has changed, but where the sporting memory remains. The central issue is simple: pioneering experiences are important, but what counts is what happens afterwards. And this is where the deputy's question is decisive: when introducing or hosting international events in the future, will there be conditions to take advantage of the experience gained in the National Games? Will there be a mechanism to facilitate authorizations and coordination with Guangdong and Hong Kong? ┬á What the Government might do ÔÇťnextÔÇŁ (realistic forecast) Without guessing too much, there are a set of steps that the Government could realistically take to transform these experiences into a permanent capacity:
ÔüáCreate an interdepartmental working group (security, traffic, tourism, sport, border issues and health), with regular meetings and a clear decision-making method. Establish a ÔÇťgreen channelÔÇŁ for ÔÇťthree-regionÔÇŁ events, with defined criteria, fixed deadlines and a predictable administrative circuit. ÔüáConsolidate an annual or multi-annual calendar with 1ÔÇô2 anchor events (e.g. a cross-border cycling race and a half marathon/border crossing race). Strengthen cooperation with Guangdong and Hong Kong in a technical format, not just political: operational protocols, contingency plans and coordination between teams on the ground. ÔüáSecure partners with operational capacity and sponsorship, including robust private sector participation, where this increases efficiency and scale.
The risk is known: everything will be left with ÔÇťgood intentionsÔÇŁ if there is no owner of the process ÔÇö a leading entity ÔÇö and execution goals with accountability. If Macau really wants to gain scale and regional relevance through sport, the path passes less through announcements and more through mechanisms, deadlines, leadership and execution. Deputy Leong Sun Iok's interpellation, in this sense, is a good starting point to ask the right question ÔÇö and, above all, to demand operational answers.

item.title: The Geopolitics of Flashpoint
item.description: ÔÇťPower is never silent; when diplomacy fails, it speaks in the language of escalation.ÔÇŁ Robert D. Kaplan ┬á The triangular relationship between the United States, Israel and the Islamic Republic of Iran has constituted, since the end of the 1970s, one of the structuring axes of international politics in the Middle East. Its evolution over almost half a century reveals a pattern of persistent antagonism, shaped by ideological, strategic, security and civilizational factors that reinforce each other. From 2020 onwards, and with greater intensity after 2022, this antagonism acquired a systemic dimension, as it began to intersect with broader global rivalries, namely the strategic competition between the United States, China and Russia and directly influence the stability of energy markets, the regional security architecture and the very credibility of international norms regarding nuclear proliferation. The genesis of this conflict dates back to the Iranian Revolution of 1979, which overthrew the regime of Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, a central ally of Washington, and established a theocratic republic based on a Shiite revolutionary ideology. The hostage crisis at the US embassy in Tehran, which lasted 444 days, crystallized a perception of irreconcilable hostility between the two states. The hostage crisis left an indelible mark on mutual perception, cementing the image of the United States as the ÔÇťGreat SatanÔÇŁ in Iranian revolutionary doctrine. This rupture was not just diplomatic, as it represented a profound reconfiguration of the regional order, by replacing a pro-Western pillar with a regime that saw itself as the vanguard of an anti-imperialist and anti-Zionist revolution. For Israel, the rise of the Islamic Republic constituted an existential threat. Until then, the Shah's Iran functioned as a strategic counterweight to Arab nationalism and Iraq's regional ambitions. From 1979 onwards, Israel began to face a State that not only rejected its legitimacy, but was ideologically committed to its elimination. The emergence of an openly anti-Zionist Islamic Republic represented an existential threat, permanently altering Israeli security calculations. This perception became even more acute with Iranian support for non-state actors such as Hezbollah in Lebanon and, later, for Palestinian groups such as Hamas and Islamic Jihad. Iranian foreign policy, especially under hard-line leadership, is based on an ideological vision that considers the international order led by the United States to be illegitimate. This vision supports the construction of the so-called ÔÇťAxis of ResistanceÔÇŁ, a network of state and non-state actors that includes Hezbollah, Shiite militias in Iraq, armed groups in Syria, the Houthis in Yemen and Palestinian factions. The objective of this network is to project influence, create strategic depth and increase the costs of any military action against Iran. The underlying logic is clear because by dispersing the battlefield across multiple theaters, Tehran seeks to compensate for its conventional inferiority vis-├á-vis the United States and Israel. On the Israeli side, the national security strategy is based on three pillars with maintaining a qualitative military advantage, preventing any regional rival from acquiring nuclear weapons and the ability to carry out preventive operations to neutralize emerging threats. This doctrine, developed since the 1960s, was reinforced after the 1973 war and consolidated with the preventive attack on the Iraqi nuclear reactor in Osirak, in 1981. From the 2000s, with the advancement of the Iranian nuclear program, Israel began to consider Iran as the main strategic threat, and the possibility of Tehran achieving latent nuclear capability became the ultimate red line. The United States, in turn, plays an ambivalent role. As a global power, Washington seeks to guarantee the stability of energy routes, contain nuclear proliferation and preserve its credibility as a guarantor of security for its regional allies. However, since the invasion of Iraq in 2003, American policy in the Middle East has been marked by internal tensions between interventionist and isolationist tendencies. The withdrawal from the nuclear agreement (JCPOA) in 2018, followed by years of economic sanctions and indirect confrontations, contributed to an environment of deep distrust between Washington and Tehran. Between 2020 and 2025, the region saw an intensification of the so-called ÔÇťwar in the gray zoneÔÇŁ, characterized by cyber attacks, industrial sabotage, clandestine operations and clashes through proxies. This phase can be considered as a period in which the conflict remains mainly in the shadows, with both sides seeking to inflict costs without crossing the threshold of direct confrontation. This dynamic reflects a logic of mutual deterrence, as each actor seeks to avoid an open war, but also to avoid appearing weak before their adversaries and their internal audiences. As of 2024, several factors have contributed to increasing the risk of escalation. Firstly, the Iranian nuclear program has advanced significantly, approaching levels of enrichment and technical capacity that, according to Western assessments, could allow a rapid breakout. Second, the growing assertiveness of Hezbollah and Iraqi militias has increased pressure on Israel and American forces in the region. From mid-2025, signs of accelerated deterioration became evident. Israel has intensified clandestine operations against Iranian scientists and facilities; Iran responded with cyber attacks and strengthening Hezbollah's military capabilities. In the Gulf, incidents involving commercial ships and drones have increased tension between Tehran and Washington. Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates, despite efforts to reach diplomatic relations with Iran, remained deeply concerned about Iranian expansionism and the possibility of a regional arms race. In this context, the end of February 2026 emerged as a turning point. The combination of advances in Iran's nuclear program, proxy attacks against American interests, and Israeli fears that the window for preemptive action was closing created an environment in which deterrence began to fail. Deterrence theory teaches that this failure occurs when the threat of punishment no longer outweighs the perceived benefit of aggressive action. In the Iranian case, the perception that obtaining a latent nuclear capability could guarantee the regime's survival became a powerful incentive to resist Western pressure. ┬á (Continues)

item.title: Elegy for a discreet sinologist
item.description: Whenever I met him, he told me about the poem about the pig, by Paul Claudel, which was in the book Connaissance de lÔÇÖest. He no longer owned that book, which he had sold most of his library in second-hand raids, as many other less well-off poets also did. He confessed to me, like someone crossing the street, that he had also sold mine. I had found it ÔÇťpolitical and sadÔÇŁ, my book of verses about Macau, which has a name similar to mold. He told me this on a postcard with a tiny font, which I keep like someone who keeps an unpublished verse from the Old Testament. Signed: Gil de Carvalho. At the time he seemed to live with little, accompanied by friends and some dead people, as we later learned. That they were fine, he said, that they were doing well, he said: after all, they were dead. There was a dry man in him, very affable, who spoke to everyone. I found him in Havaneza de S├úo Domingos, where people and some of the last surviving literati from the 19th century pass by. When the conversation turned to contemporary Greek, or English, or Portuguese poetry, he spoke in a lively, even enthusiastic way, showing a mouth full of small teeth. He was an old-fashioned man, without a cell phone or email, who wrote by hand in irreproachably illegible handwriting, very choppy and faded, like his poetry. This was, in fact, dry too, like lands from which water retreats. But then we look and see many layers, many strata ÔÇö while it ends up in alien landscapes, cut only by an occasional flurry of commas. He gave me a mission: to create a Portuguese magazine about Asia ÔÇö perhaps the existence of the Via do Meio, which he never knew about, would have dissuaded him from this project? ÔÇô something I will never do. I was reminded of such an exotic request (which brought together our common ÔÇťOrientalistÔÇŁ interests) the romantic projects of the newspaper Independente, of a world of typographies, publishers, cafes, brief and whimsical publications that was still his world. The world where telephones are landlines; more than fixed, still and even silent, as the speech was only to be transmitted to another face. It made him a little disgusted when I pushed these ideas away. I replied that what I wanted was to republish A dama luminosa ÔÇö his fundamental book of essays on China ÔÇö to pull the rug out from under Portuguese sinology. I would never agree with such banality, if I told him so, but (his) poetry would be a ÔÇťscience of lookingÔÇŁ, crossing cultures and languages, embracing the unfamiliar. His emotional territory ÔÇö Algarve, T├╝rkiye, China ÔÇö was rare and did not fit into the well-known maps. It wasn't Paris. It was towards Serra dÔÇÖOssa, towards Izmir, towards the world of Islamicized Chinese, that she was heading. It stops around, in waves, like breathing. The reports of his death say he was a sinologist, and even an orientalist, not just a poet. It could be, as in fact Gil de Carvalho participated as a translator and organizer in projects linked to classical Chinese poetry in Portuguese. An Anthology of Chinese Poetry (Lisbon: Ass├şrio & Alvim, 2nd edition, 2010) is his pinnacle. But Gil's affectionate sinology, humble and discreet, was intended to be investigative, unlike the sinological boasting of others. It is remarkable today how some descriptions he made of classical Chinese poetry also serve his own work: literature that prefers the polis (the community) to the soul, in some way a recording and exploration of material and socio-cultural strata: ÔÇťmaterialist but leaving out a certain visibleÔÇŁ (p. 8 of The Luminous Lady). But my favorite piece of his ÔÇťsyniqueÔÇŁ is his succinct, hyper-anthological collection of anonymous poems from diverse traditions. The precious little book is called Anonymous Poems: Turks, Mongols, Chinese and Uncertain, by Ass├şrio & Alvim. Gil reminds me so much of those uncertainties, those difficult ones, those brief ones. Those who escape through half-open borders.

item.title: Learning from the experience of comparative law to consolidate a legal barrier for State Security
item.description: Learning from the experience of comparative law to consolidate a legal barrier for State Security Ao Chi Tan On February 10, 2026, the Legislative Assembly of the Macau Special Administrative Region unanimously approved, in its generality, the proposed law entitled ÔÇťCommission for the Defense of State Security of the Macau Special Administrative RegionÔÇŁ , marking a further improvement in Macau's top structure in protecting State Security. Currently, the global security situation is increasingly complex and Macau, being a highly open region, faces diverse security challenges. This legislation represents both a synthesis and refinement of previous State Security practice, and an important measure to proactively align with the rules of modern State Security governance. The creation of a specialized coordinating body, accompanied by detailed judicial processes, is already a common practice adopted by several countries in the protection of State Security. With regard to the trial processes of sensitive cases, all countries follow the principle that public trial is the rule and closed-door trial is the exception, developing institutional regimes with their own characteristics: ÔÇô The United States has established a tiered closure system, applying, under the Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure and the Classified Information Prosecution Act, rigorous secret trials or hearings in State Security cases, giving precedence to the protection of confidential secrets. ÔÇô The United Kingdom has instituted a special closed materials process, allowing courts to use, in State Security cases, confidential materials not disclosed to the defendant or ordinary lawyers, with the participation of special defenders authorized after security checks in the closed trial phases. ÔÇô Germany assigns exclusive jurisdiction over serious State Security crimes and terrorism to the Federal Attorney General, with the parts of the trial involving confidential information taking place behind closed doors, in accordance with the law. ÔÇô In France, cases of absolute confidentiality in matters of State Security, such as espionage and disclosure of defense secrets, fall under the exclusive jurisdiction of the State Security Section of the Paris Court of Appeal, with a full trial in camera and access to the files limited to specific judicial personnel and verified defenders. ÔÇô In Japan, State Security cases involving defense secrets fall under the exclusive jurisdiction of the Tokyo High Court, with a full trial in camera. The diverse institutional practices of various countries provide a valuable reference for Macau in regulating the trial of confidential cases and improving procedural justice. When it comes to regulating the practice of law, all countries adopt strict regimes for controlling confidential information, simultaneously balancing the guarantee of the right to defense and the protection of State Security: ÔÇô In the United States, lawyers participating in State Security cases must undergo a maximum federal security check, and judges can legally limit their right to visit and consult documents to prevent information leaks. ÔÇô In the United Kingdom, only special defenders authorized by the Attorney General can have access to confidential materials in State Security cases. ÔÇô Germany provides that the Federal Office for the Protection of the Constitution carries out mandatory security checks on defenders in State Security cases who have access to confidential information, with those who are not approved not being allowed to consult confidential evidence. ÔÇô In France, defenders in State Security cases must undergo a security check by the Ministry of Defense and sign a lifelong confidentiality agreement and cannot transmit confidential evidence to the client. ÔÇô In Japan, defenders with access to ÔÇťdefense secretsÔÇŁ must be verified by the Ministry of Defense and sign a commitment to protect secrets, under penalty of a lifetime ban from practicing law and a possible prison sentence. Macau's understanding of international experiences is always based on the principle of ÔÇťrooting them in local reality and serving our own endsÔÇŁ, not consisting of a mere transposition. Unlike the risk of misuse of power that affects some State Security bodies in other countries, the Macau State Security Defense Commission operates strictly within the framework of the Constitution and the Basic Law, under the direction and supervision of the Chief Executive. By absorbing judicial experiences from different countries, Macau combines the characteristics of its own legal system, retaining and applying the fundamental principles of legal competence, procedural transparency and guarantee of rights, consolidating the line of defense of State Security and fully ensuring the legitimate rights of Macau residents. Acting lawyer

item.title: Art without borders | Registration open for ÔÇťWorkshops for ChildrenÔÇŁ
item.description: Registration is open until March 22 to participate in seven sessions of artistic workshops, aimed at children and young people aged 4 to 16, under the ÔÇťArt Without Borders ÔÇô Workshops for ChildrenÔÇŁ program that takes place between April and June at the Macau Museum of Art (MAM). The activities are exclusive to ÔÇťFriends of MAMÔÇŁ, that is, you must join the club. Registration for workshops and membership of the ÔÇťFriends of MAMÔÇŁ can be done through the Macau Single Account. Each workshop lasts 12 hours and costs 240 patacas. The places that will host these activities are Espa├žo Zero, at MAM and the Art Square at the Museum of Gifts on the Transfer of Sovereignty of Macau. The Cultural Institute points out that the organization of these events aims to encourage young people ÔÇťto observe and imagine traditional aesthetics, as well as to develop creative practice in contemporary art through different forms of expression, allowing them to experience the infinite possibilities of artÔÇŁ. In conjunction with the theme of the exhibition dedicated to landscape painting from the Ming and Qing dynasties, this series of workshops will take children and young people of different ages ÔÇťon an artistic journey through time, following the trail of the ancient masters of Chinese paintingÔÇŁ. ÔÇťPainting and Imagination ÔÇô Creative Art Workshop for ChildrenÔÇŁ, conducted in Cantonese or English (a total of five sessions), was designed for children aged between 5 and 10 and will have as its theme natural landscapes that will be reinterpreted through paintings with Indian ink and collages. The ÔÇťImaginary Landscape Painting ÔÇô Creative Workshop for Young PeopleÔÇŁ workshop is aimed at teenagers aged 11 to 16. Finally, a workshop will be organized for parents and children aged between 4 and 6.

item.title: IC | April will feature a comedy festival, reading promotion and sports carnival
item.description: With the Macau International Parade scheduled for the end of the month, Macau is preparing to organize a series of events in April, including the third edition of the Macau International Comedy Festival, with activities in Macau and Hengqin. The Cultural Institute is also preparing reading month for April Although the March procession is still taking place in the churchyard, the Cultural Institute (IC) is dealing with ÔÇťlarge-scale eventsÔÇŁ that will affect Macau's cultural agenda in April, a poster that was on the table at the meeting of the Consultative Council for Cultural Development, which took place on Tuesday. Next month, the third edition of the Macau International Comedy Festival will be organized, with an extension to Hengqin, complying with the ÔÇťÔÇśOne Festival, Two LocationsÔÇÖ formatÔÇŁ. Still far from presenting the poster, the IC points out that the comedy festival will be an ÔÇťinternational level eventÔÇŁ that will culturally enrich the Greater Bay Area and ÔÇťpromote regional cultural exchangeÔÇŁ. Fulfilling the objective of taking advantage of sport to create cultural and tourist attractions, the IC will launch the ÔÇťSports and Cultural CarnivalÔÇŁ in April, which will provide ÔÇťexperiences with cultural and sporting themes held in World Heritage sites and in communitiesÔÇŁ. The event will include the traditional Dragon Boat Regatta. In another aspect, the IC will focus on promoting literacy, designating April as the ÔÇťMonth of Reading Together throughout the City of MacauÔÇŁ, in collaboration with the Directorate of Education and Youth Development Services, the Institute of Social Action and civil organizations. As such, ÔÇťa series of activities to promote reading will be organized during the month of April, with the aim of creating a good reading environment for everyone and deepening the construction of a ÔÇśCity of ReadingÔÇÖ, points out the IC. It is important to highlight that the month of reading follows national and international initiatives, with the Central Government designating the fourth week of April each year as ÔÇťReading Week for AllÔÇŁ, in addition to the celebration of World Book Day on April 23rd. ┬á Make the future Another of the points discussed at the meeting in terms of events was the Macau 2026 International Parade, which takes advantage of ÔÇťthe uniqueness of the fusion between East and WestÔÇŁ of the territory. The parade, which takes place on March 29th, this year has the theme ÔÇťThe Maritime Silk Road as a bridge for cultural exchangeÔÇŁ. The event will bring together ÔÇťartistic groups from all over the world and local artists who will dance and sing in dazzling costumes through the busy streets, showcasing the unique character of the Historic Center of MacauÔÇŁ, predicts the IC. The agenda of the meeting also included the presentation of the ÔÇťTraining Program for Young Macau MusiciansÔÇŁ organized by the Macau Orchestra Society, which aims to train musical talents and provide a platform for participation in professional orchestras. The training program also aims to increase employment opportunities for Macau's musical talents in professional orchestras, through the study of Western instruments or traditional Chinese music, contributing to building a reserve of local artistic talents.

item.title: ÔÇťBeat. RespirationÔÇŁ, a concert to listen to at CCM this Friday
item.description: The Macau Chinese Orchestra (OCM) takes the stage this Friday in the small auditorium of the Macau Cultural Center (CCM) as part of the show ÔÇťBatida. Respira├ž├úoÔÇŁ, which highlights ÔÇťthe unique fascination of wind and percussion instrumentsÔÇŁ. According to a note from the Cultural Institute (IC), this Friday's show, which starts at 7:45 pm, will be directed by the OCM's musical director and main conductor, Zhang Lie, and will feature the participation of dizi performer, Zhang Weiliang, as artistic director. ÔÇťA skillful dialogue between the orchestraÔÇÖs wind and percussion sectionsÔÇŁ is promised in this concert, which features ÔÇťa mix of resonant percussion and fluid winds, revealing the fascination of Chinese instrumental musicÔÇŁ. Zhang Weiliang is described as a ÔÇťtrue master of stage performance, composition and music education, as well as a pioneer of ÔÇśnew folk musicÔÇÖÔÇŁ. Furthermore, he is a personality from the world of music who has performed ÔÇťsome of the soundtracks most beloved by the publicÔÇŁ for classic Chinese films, such as ÔÇťGoodbye My ConcubineÔÇŁ, ÔÇťJu DouÔÇŁ and ÔÇťRed LanternÔÇŁ. Traditional compliment The concert will consist of a repertoire of traditional folk songs and modern pieces with new musical arrangements, including compositions such as ÔÇťHanging the Red LanternsÔÇŁ, ÔÇťFestival das LanternasÔÇŁ, ÔÇťDespedida FestivaÔÇŁ, ÔÇťL├ígrimas pela Flores e Felicita├ž├ÁesÔÇŁ, which make their debut in the territory. The OCM wind and percussion groups will also present ÔÇťO Rio Amarelo Jorra do C├ęuÔÇŁ, an original piece by Zhang Lie, while the OCM suona performer, Tian Ding, will perform ÔÇťAo Nascer do SolÔÇŁ, a work of his own. Tickets are now on sale and cost between 150 and 200 patacas. This concert is part of the OCM 2025-26 Concert Season.

item.title: Exhibition | The passion for art by Lu├şs Pessanha, Chao Iok Leng and Jos├ę Duarte
